content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                                    Date : Sept. 19, 1979


                                                            M 79-136
From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Ted Prim

Subject: Candidate Statements


     Government Code Section 87201 requires persons for offices
specified in Section 87200 to file a Statement of Economic Interests
with their declaration of candidacy. A candidate will have complied
with this requirement if he or she files a Statement of Economic
Interests at the time of or prior to the time of the filing of the
final document with state or local election officials which is
necessary to have his name listed on the ballot or to have write-
in votes counted on his behalf.

     This interpretation is consistent with the Commission's defini-
tion of "declaration of candidacy" in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18902
as it relates to the prohibition contained in Government Code Section
89001.

Attachment
TP:nrw

State of California

Memorandum

To : Ted                                          Date : July 24, 1979

     cc: Bob S.      Bill
         Lee         Alice
         Sarah       Helen
         Dwight

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Gina T.

Subject: Candidate's Statements of Economic Interests

The following problem has been brought to my attention by Yvonne in the
San Diego County Elections Department:

In the past when a person was running for public office he or she had a certain
30 day period in which to pick up the nominating papers and Statement of Economic
Interests (Form 720) from the County Clerk, complete them, and return them. It
is the county clerk's policy not to accept a declaration of candidacy without a
completed Form 720 because of Government Code Section D1720l which states that a
candidate "...shall file with his declaration of candidacy a statement disclosing
his investments and his interests in real property."

The Secretary of State has recently amended the Elections Code requiring candidates
to complete their nominating papers in the clerk's office. The papers cannot
leave the clerk's office under any circumstances. It is very difficult or impos-
sible for many candidates to complete the Form 720 without referring to other
records, etc...

Yvonne asked the following questions:

1. Given the recent changes in the Elections Code is it legal for her to
   allow a candidate to complete the Form 720 outside her office?

2. If so, should it be completed prior to filing a declaration of candidacy
   so that they can be filed together or can she accept the declaration of
   candidacy without a Form 720, as long as the 720 is filed within the 30
   day nominating period?

Please let me know as soon as possible.     Thanks.

sections.question:
1. Given the recent changes in the Elections Code is it legal for her to allow a candidate to complete the Form 720 outside her office?

2. If so, should it be completed prior to filing a declaration of candidacy so that they can be filed together or can she accept the declaration of candidacy without a Form 720, as long as the 720 is filed within the 30 day nominating period?
sections.conclusion: A candidate will have complied with this requirement if he or she files a Statement of Economic Interests at the time of or prior to the time of the filing of the final document with state or local election officials which is necessary to have his name listed on the ballot or to have write-in votes counted on his behalf.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether county election officials may allow candidates to complete the Statement of Economic Interests (Form 720) outside the clerk's office given recent Elections Code amendments requiring nominating papers to be completed in the clerk's office, and the timing requirements for filing the Form 720 relative to the declaration of candidacy.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that candidates may file the Statement of Economic Interests prior to or at the same time as filing the final document necessary for ballot inclusion, rather than strictly requiring it to accompany the declaration of candidacy. This interpretation allows flexibility in timing while maintaining compliance with Government Code Section 87201.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of two memoranda: the initial request from Gina T. (July 24, 1979) and the FPPC response from Ted Prim (Sept. 19, 1979). The response addresses the timing requirement rather than explicitly answering whether the form can be completed off-site. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers; extracted text is synthesized from the substantive portions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Given the recent changes in the Elections Code is it legal for her to allow a candidate to complete the Form 720 outside her office?

2. If so, should it be completed prior to filing a declaration of candidacy so that they can be filed together or can she accept the declaration of candidacy without a Form 720, as long as the 720 is filed within the 30 day nominating period?

CONCLUSION: A candidate will have complied with this requirement if he or she files a Statement of Economic Interests at the time of or prior to the time of the filing of the final document with state or local election officials which is necessary to have his name listed on the ballot or to have write-in votes counted on his behalf.

embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum addressing whether candidates can complete the Statement of Economic Interests form outside the county clerk's office when recent Elections Code amendments require nominating papers to be completed on-site. The Commission provides guidance that the form must be filed by the time of or before the final ballot documentation is submitted.
